data.title: Restaurant check: Fast-growing Jersey Mike's preps for an IPO
data.description:
Jersey Mike's appears to be pushing forward with its IPO plans.

Sources told Bloomberg that the company is working with Morgan Stanley (MS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Jefferies Financial (JEF) to explore an IPO that would value the company well above $12B. Notably, Blackstone (BX) paid $8B for a majority stake completed in early 2025.

Jersey Mike's traces its roots to 1956, when the original Mike’s Subs opened in Point Pleasant, New Jersey. Founder Peter Cancro bought that store and began to franchise the chain in the late 1980s. From the original single store, Jersey Mike’s has scaled to operate over 3,200 sandwich shops across the U.S.  and Canada. It is largely recognized as one of the fastest-growing fast-casual brands. Importantly, consumer surveys point to strong name recognition and favorability for the chain.

Looking ahead, Jersey Mike’s has announced a major international push, signing a franchise agreement to open 400 stores in the United Kingdom and Ireland as part of a broader global expansion strategy. A Jersey Mike's IPO could help fund the international expansion plans while providing investors exposure to a scaled franchisor model with recurring royalty streams and asset-light economics.

Jersey Mike's competes directly with Jimmy John’s, Firehouse Subs, Potbelly (PBPB), Quiznos, and Penn Station East Coast Subs. On a more indirect basis, Jersey Mike's scraps for lunch traffic with Panera (PANERA), Portillo's (PTLO), McDonald's (MCD), Wendy's (WEN), and Burger King (QSR).

data.title: Adtalem raises adjusted EPS guidance to 17–20% growth while signaling continued enrollment momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Adtalem Global Education (ATGE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Stephen Beard, CEO & Chairman, highlighted "our 10th consecutive quarter of enrollment growth," and confirmed that Adtalem remains "on track to achieve our full year revenue guidance of 6% to 8.5% growth," while raising adjusted earnings per share guidance to "17% to 20% growth." Beard emphasized, "We deployed $165 million to share repurchases, and we have approximately $728 million remaining on our current authorization." He pointed to sustainable momentum and the company's "differentiated business model," further noting, "Walden has achieved record total enrollment. More than 52,000 students now generate industry-leading scale and operating leverage." Beard also reported Chamberlain "growing enrollment by 6,000 students in just 3 years to reach a record of 40,000 students." He announced an upcoming Investor Day where the company will present its "multiyear growth framework, including capacity expansion plans and new revenue streams."
  • Beard stated, "Total enrollment grew over 6% to 97,000 students. Revenue grew 12% to $503 million. We delivered further efficiencies as adjusted EBITDA grew to $155 million." He added, "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.43, an increase of 34% versus last year."
  • Robert Phelan, Senior VP & CFO, said, "Halfway through fiscal year 2026, we are executing against our Growth with Purpose strategy, putting us on track to meet our full year financial goals." Phelan continued, "Revenue in the second quarter increased by 12.4% to $503.4 million, driven by all 3 segments." He added, "Walden continues to be a source of strength and in particular, was aided by a 1-week academic calendar shift from the third quarter into the second quarter this fiscal year, resulting in an incremental $18 million in revenue recognized in Q2 rather than in Q3." Phelan also stated, "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA came in at $154.9 million, up 23.9% compared to the prior year."

Outlook

  • Phelan provided updated guidance: "We are maintaining our annual revenue guidance" and stated, "We are raising our adjusted EPS guidance from the previous range of $7.60 to $7.90 or growth of 14% to 18.5% to a range of $7.80 to $8 or growth of 17% to 20%. At the midpoint, our adjusted EPS range is increasing by $0.15." He noted, "The increase in adjusted EPS guidance contemplates our continued commitment to expanding our fiscal year 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin by approximately 100 basis points." Beard reinforced that Chamberlain's "application momentum will translate into new enrollment growth and position us well heading into the critical fall cycle."

Financial Results

  • Adtalem reported Q2 revenue of $503.4 million, with Walden contributing $217.6 million and Chamberlain $183.8 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $154.9 million, with a margin of 30.8%. Adjusted operating income reached $126.1 million, while adjusted net income was $87.9 million. Adjusted earnings per share came to $2.43. The company repurchased 1.7 million shares at an average price of $95, completing a prior authorization and leaving $728 million available under a new $750 million program.
  • Walden enrollment was up 13%, reaching 52,400 students. Chamberlain reported a 1% decline in total student enrollment for the quarter, but pre-licensure BSN programs have grown for 14 consecutive quarters. The Medical and Veterinary segment posted revenue of $102 million, with an adjusted EBITDA of $31.4 million.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: "Even excluding the calendar shift, the Walden numbers continue to impress. Can we just double-click on that? What exactly is going on? Where are you seeing the growth? Do you think you're taking share from other schools in this market?" Beard answered that "the Walden growth is consistent across the board, but we see it most pronounced in the areas that we've consistently been most excited about. That's in the behavioral sciences programs and also in the nursing program and the MSN credential."
  • Silber followed up on regulatory changes regarding loan caps and the Sallie Mae partnership. Beard replied, "We're working with Sallie Mae on definitive documentation for that partnership... we continue to be excited about that partnership and what it means for the entire portfolio, including the medical and veterinary segment."
  • Jack Slevin, Jefferies: Asked about Chamberlain's application trends and enrollment lead time, Beard said, "We identified a couple of gaps, marketing effectiveness and enrollment funnel conversion. We took a number of steps to address processes on both dimensions. As I said in the prepared remarks, some of the leading indicators of enrollment have been really positive. Application volumes up for both pre-licensure and post-licensure nursing programs." Slevin also questioned the revenue improvement scenario at the high end of guidance. Beard indicated, "It would come from a quicker-than-anticipated return to form at Chamberlain and potentially some additional acceleration in the trajectory of the Med/Vet segment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on underlying drivers of segment strength and sustainability, with a generally positive but probing tone, particularly regarding Chamberlain's recovery and Walden's continued momentum. Silber's questions were detailed but constructive, and Slevin's inquiries probed for clarity on strategic execution and upside potential.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone. Beard used phrases such as "we feel really good about the implications of those moves" and "we are confident in the trajectory of the recovery." Phelan reinforced this with "we are executing against our Growth with Purpose strategy, putting us on track to meet our full year financial goals."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady and constructive, though with slightly more emphasis on operational recovery at Chamberlain and a firmer stance on raising EPS guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its adjusted EPS guidance from $7.60–$7.90 to $7.80–$8, targeting 17% to 20% growth, compared to the previous guidance of 14% to 18.5% growth. Revenue guidance was reiterated at 6% to 8.5% growth.
  • Chamberlain's enrollment softness, highlighted last quarter as execution-related, persisted but was counterbalanced by operational improvements and rising application volumes. The focus on marketing and funnel conversion continued, with management expressing increased confidence in recovery as the year progresses.
  • Walden's momentum remained robust, with double-digit enrollment growth and new program launches contributing to results. The Medical and Veterinary segment sustained stable performance, with efficiency improvements driving margin gains.
  • Analyst focus shifted from concerns about Chamberlain's marketing execution and tech infrastructure last quarter to a more forward-looking view on segment contributions and upside potential in the current period. Management's tone was consistent, with a slightly greater emphasis on operational discipline and future growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Beard acknowledged that Chamberlain's "Q3 total enrollment will remain soft as recent improvements work their way through the student journey, but the real proof point is the fall enrollment cycle."
  • Phelan described "higher provision for income taxes" and the impact of a "1-week academic calendar shift" as notable factors affecting quarterly performance, while reiterating that the shift "has no net impact on our annual performance."
  • No new regulatory or competitive threats were highlighted, though continued execution on marketing and enrollment funnel improvements at Chamberlain remains a key focus area for risk mitigation.

Final Takeaway

Adtalem's second quarter showcased continued enrollment momentum, robust financial results, and a disciplined capital allocation approach, punctuated by a raised adjusted EPS growth target of 17% to 20%. Management signaled confidence in operational improvements at Chamberlain, record enrollments at Walden, and sustainable growth prospects across segments, with a multiyear growth framework and new revenue streams to be detailed at the upcoming Investor Day.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BXP outlines 4% occupancy growth target by 2027 amid strong leasing and $1.25B in asset sales progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BXP, Inc. (BXP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Owen Thomas, CEO & Chairman of the Board, reported that BXP had "a very strong year of performance in 2025 in all areas critical to our business, namely leasing, asset sales, development starts and deliveries, financing and client service, notwithstanding our below reforecast FFO per share outcome for the fourth quarter." He highlighted progress in leasing, asset sales, deleveraging, development, and capital raising, with over 1.8 million square feet leased in the fourth quarter and 5.5 million square feet for the year, "well above our goals for the year." Thomas confirmed BXP is ahead or on track with the business plan outlined at the September investor conference, reiterating confidence in achieving a "4% occupancy gain over the next 2 years."
  • Asset sales remain central, with 12 assets sold for net proceeds of over $1 billion in 2025 and January, and 8 more under contract for $230 million in 2026. "In total, we have 21 transactions closed or well underway with estimated net proceeds of roughly $1.25 billion." Thomas stated, "we have exited the Life Science business on the West Coast, but remain committed to the sector through our substantial life science holdings in the Boston region."
  • New development is focused on pre-leased office opportunities and multifamily projects with equity partners. Thomas detailed the pre-leased 2100 M Street project in Washington, D.C., and progress with 343 Madison Avenue in New York, where "we finalized a lease commitment with Starr for 29% of the space...and are negotiating a letter of intent for another 16% of the building."
  • Michael LaBelle, Executive VP, Treasurer & CFO, stated: "For 2025, we reported total consolidated revenues of $3.5 billion and full year FFO of $1.2 billion or $6.85 per share. Our fourth quarter FFO was $1.76 per share, and it came in short of the midpoint of our guidance by $0.05 per share due primarily to higher-than-anticipated G&A expense and noncash reserves for accrued rental income."

Outlook

  • LaBelle introduced 2026 FFO guidance in a range of $6.88 to $7.04 per share, "which is within consensus estimates. The midpoint of our guidance for FFO was $6.96 per share, and it represents an increase of $0.11 per share from 2025." Management expects occupancy to climb from 86.7% at year-end 2025 to approximately 89% by the end of 2026, with guidance for same-property NOI growth of 1.25% to 2.25%. LaBelle noted, "2026 represents a return to FFO growth for BXP."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenues for 2025 were $3.5 billion. Full year FFO totaled $1.2 billion or $6.85 per share, with Q4 FFO at $1.76 per share. LaBelle attributed the Q4 miss to "higher-than-anticipated G&A expense and noncash reserves for accrued rental income." Gains on sale in Q4 totaled $208 million on $890 million of asset sales, with net proceeds from sales activity at $800 million. The company ended 2025 with $1.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents.
  • 2026 guidance assumes additional asset sales of $360 million, resulting in a reduction in portfolio NOI from 2025 to 2026 of $70 million to $74 million. Net interest expense is projected to decrease by $38 million to $48 million in 2026.

Q&A

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked about the balance between accelerating dispositions and FFO growth. Thomas replied, "we're sticking with our forecast, we might sell more. We're paying attention to the dilutive impacts, but we're also paying attention to optimizing our portfolio and deleveraging and creating capital for our development program."
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked about conversion rates for leasing negotiations. Douglas Linde, President & Director, confirmed "on the 1.1 million, it's actually now at 1.2 million as of late last night of deals that are in 'lease' negotiation, I think our conversion rate is like 95%."
  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Inquired about AI's impact on space needs. Thomas said, "AI so far for BXP's footprint has been a net plus, not a negative because we've had very significant AI leasing...particularly in the Bay Area, which is an important market."
  • John Kim, BMO: Sought clarity on FFO growth cadence. LaBelle responded, "We expect our kind of in-service occupancy from the same-property portfolio will be flat in the first quarter, and then the occupancy will build after that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on dilution from asset sales, leasing conversion rates, AI's impact on demand, and the cadence of FFO growth, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for risks and upside in leasing and portfolio strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone, repeatedly expressing optimism about leasing demand, asset sales execution, and strategic flexibility. Thomas stated, "we are very much on track executing our business plan as outlined last September."
  • Compared to last quarter, the sentiment remains confident, with management providing more detailed progress updates and reaffirming the business plan despite a fourth quarter FFO miss.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 FFO is now $6.88 to $7.04 per share, with an expected occupancy increase to 89% by year-end 2026, compared to a previous projection of 88.3%. The business plan and asset sales target ($1.9 billion by 2028) remain consistent but are ahead of schedule, with $1.25 billion in progress.
  • Management's tone remains confident and forward-looking, with leasing and asset sales outpacing prior targets. Analyst questions continue to focus on dilution, leasing conversion, and market risks, similar to the prior call.
  • Strategic focus has shifted further toward premier workplace assets, multifamily development, and exiting West Coast life science holdings.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited higher G&A costs and noncash reserves for accrued rent as factors in the Q4 FFO miss.
  • Analysts raised concerns about dilution from asset sales and the impact of AI and job cuts on office demand.
  • Management addressed these by emphasizing accretive land sales, robust leasing momentum, and proactive responses to market changes, including shifting development capital to multifamily and premier workplaces.

Final Takeaway

BXP management emphasized strong leasing performance, accelerated asset sales, and successful execution of its business plan, including a target of 4% occupancy growth over the next two years and a return to FFO growth in 2026. The company continues to optimize its portfolio by concentrating on premier workplace assets, expanding multifamily development with partners, and actively pursuing value-creative asset dispositions. Management expressed continued confidence in sustaining growth and deleveraging, supported by higher occupancy, robust leasing pipelines, and active capital management initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: New York regulator pushes back against Williams' Constitution pipeline proposal
data.description:
New York's environmental regulator said Wednesday it filed with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to oppose the Williams-led (WMB) effort to revive a permit to build the previously canceled Constitution Pipeline project from Pennsylvania to New York.

Constitution recently petitioned FERC to reissue a required permit for construction of the pipeline, but New York's Department of Environmental Conservation said in its filing to FERC that it opposed the request, saying the move would override the state agency's oversight authority under the Clean Water Act.

The New York regulator said it did not waive its Clean Water Act Section 401 authority and will explore all available options to vigorously defend the state's rights.

Williams (WMB) canceled the Constitution project in 2020 and the Northeast Supply Enhancement Project in 2024 after years of fighting for permits from state regulators; the state regulator approved a water permit for the NESE project in November.

Last May, the Trump administration used New York's reconsideration of Williams' (WMB) proposed pipelines in the state as part of a deal with Governor Hochul to lift a federal ban on the construction of the Empire Wind project off the coast of New York.

data.title: Amazing Abundance: Tesla's robot-fueled vision for the future
data.description:
Branded as “Amazing Abundance,” Tesla’s (TSLA) new mission captures Elon Musk’s optimism—and his expectation that Optimus robots could eventually reshape U.S. economic output.

On the company’s earnings call with analysts, Musk laid out his vision for the company as it transitions from a purely auto manufacturing entity to a leading manufacturer of advanced and humanoid robots.

“Optimus will be a very capable robot,” Musk said on the call, confident that the humanoid robot could ultimately have a “meaningful” impact on U.S. GDP, as well as Tesla’s ability to ultimately produce 1M units per year

To achieve this ambitious goal, the company is sunsetting the Model S and Model Y vehicles next quarter given the increased focus on autonomy. Consequently, Tesla is converting the Fremont factory for Optimus production.

Concerning the deployment of the robotaxi, Musk dismissed reports that robotaxis in Austin are being followed by a monitoring vehicle, repeating several times through the call that “no one is following the car.”

“The level of autonomy will increase dramatically every three months,” Musk said, with the cars currently operating at 100% fully self-driving. Eventually, only a fraction of miles driven will not be autonomous, as CFO Vaibhav Taneja added that autonomy software will “be the driver of growth [for Tesla] from now on.”

To finance the development of the Optimus robot, FSD, and the Cybercab, as well as a $2B investment in xAi, Tesla (TSLA) projects capex to be in excess of $20B this year.

Taneja cautioned that the biggest constraint globally, however, continues to be on the battery pack. Additionally, he expects the company will experience margin compression in the energy business from increased low-cost competition.

Musk ended his statement reiterating the goal of achieving “amazing abundance” and creating value for the company, not by solving the easy problems, but by solving the hard ones.

“The future is more exciting than you can imagine.”

data.title: Ethan Allen targets growth with 25% increase in digital marketing amid tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ethan Allen Interiors Inc. (ETD) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO M. Kathwari explained that second quarter results were strongly impacted by "the government shutdown, resulting in lower consumer confidence, lower traffic to our design centers and lower orders at retail and especially from the U.S. government contract, also impacted by a very strong previous year comparison." He added, "The good news is that we have started the third quarter with stronger traffic and positive written sales in January."
  • Kathwari emphasized, "During the last few years, we have made major changes to our vertically integrated structure, including our retail network, manufacturing, marketing, logistics and are positioned well."
  • CFO Matthew McNulty stated, "Our financial performance in the just completed second quarter was highlighted by a robust balance sheet and strong margins despite a challenging environment. Our consolidated net sales of $149.9 million benefited from a higher starting retail backlog, a higher average ticket price, incremental clearance sales and fewer returns. These increases were offset by fewer contract sales and lower demand."
  • McNulty noted, "We are pleased to see positive written order growth in January. We ended the quarter with wholesale backlog of $49.8 million, a lower volume of contract orders combined with improved customer lead times helped reduce our backlog."
  • Kathwari described product initiatives: "Our design centers have started to receive our new products introduced in the fall of last year. Our products continue to be developed under the umbrella of classics with a modern perspective. About 75% of our furniture is made in our manufacturing workshops in North America, and all products are custom-made."
  • Kathwari highlighted a 25% increase in advertising spend, "mostly in digital mediums," and noted, "While we did not get the full benefit in our second quarter of this increased marketing spend due to economic slowdown, we feel it will benefit us in the future."

Outlook

  • Management reported that "positive written order growth in January" was seen following the government shutdown, indicating an improved demand environment at the start of the third quarter.
  • Kathwari stated, "We have an opportunity of maintaining" strong margins due to investments in talent, technology, and manufacturing, though emphasized volumes are an important factor.
  • McNulty explained, "We will continue to review pricing and we'll respond quickly and thoughtfully as conditions evolve."

Financial Results

  • McNulty reported, "Retail written orders declined 17.9%, while wholesale orders were 19.3% lower than a year ago, with both metrics declining sequentially throughout the quarter as our prior year comparables got tougher."
  • Consolidated gross margin was 60.9%, up 60 basis points from a year ago, attributed to "a change in sales mix, reduced headcount, a higher average ticket price and lower inbound freight, partially offset by increased promotional activity, incremental tariffs and elevated clearance sales."
  • Adjusted operating income reached $13.5 million with an operating margin of 9%. Adjusted diluted EPS was $0.44.
  • Cash and investments at quarter end were $179.3 million with no debt. Operating cash flow deficit for the quarter was $1.8 million, attributed to changes in working capital and payroll timing.
  • The company declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.39 per share to be paid in February.

Q&A

  • Taylor Zick, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: Asked about retail written order trends and underlying performance during the quarter. McNulty responded that each month in Q2 "decreased by a higher percentage," averaging an 18% decrease, with October stronger and later months impacted by the government shutdown and prior year comparison.
  • Zick asked about contract business recovery post-shutdown. Kathwari indicated orders "are coming in reasonably high, but not as strong as we had last year because it is now taking the government, all the embassies all over the world, a little time to get back on."
  • Zick questioned margin sustainability. Kathwari replied, "We have a good opportunity of maintaining them because of the fact that a lot of work has been done at all levels in terms of combining great talent and technology."
  • Cristina Fernandez, Telsey Advisory Group LLC: Inquired about tariff impacts and mitigation. McNulty explained a three-pronged mitigation strategy: vendor cost sharing, supplier diversification, and retail price increases averaging 5%. He said, "Those did help mitigate some of the tariff impact. It did not do all of it... there will still be some more headwinds."
  • Fernandez asked about January trends. Kathwari attributed improvement to "better consumer attitude" and increased design center traffic.
  • Fernandez asked about future marketing spend. Kathwari indicated plans to "reduce some of our advertising expense in some other mediums" and focus more on digital, moving spend from $18 million to $9–$10 million per month for digital magazines.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about demand trends, margin sustainability, tariff impacts, and marketing efficiency, with a slightly negative tone due to sequential order declines and macro headwinds.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing a strategic long-term focus and highlighting early signs of demand recovery and strong margins, with Kathwari noting, "We are stronger today."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts were more pressing about order declines and tariff impacts, while management's tone was more focused on mitigation strategies and near-term demand improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to a focus on "positive written order growth in January" and margin maintenance in Q2, with less emphasis on broad sales growth.
  • Strategic focus moved toward digital marketing and tariff mitigation, compared to Q1’s broader investments in technology and design center expansion.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on sequential order declines, tariffs, and marketing ROI, while in Q1 questions centered on promotional activity and government contracts.
  • Key metrics changed: retail written orders swung from 5.2% growth in Q1 to a 17.9% decline in Q2; wholesale orders reversed from a 7.1% decline in Q1 to a 19.3% fall in Q2; gross margin remained strong but dipped slightly.
  • Management’s confidence remained steady, but shifted emphasis from growth to stabilization and mitigation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic headwinds, the government shutdown, lower traffic, and lower government contract orders as key challenges.
  • Tariff exposure remains significant, with new Section 232 tariffs on upholstered wood products at 25%, and ongoing exposure to IEEPA and Section 301 tariffs.
  • Mitigation strategies include cost sharing with vendors, sourcing diversification, and targeted price increases. Kathwari mentioned the potential upside if IEEPA tariffs are lifted, though plans do not rely on this outcome.
  • Analysts expressed concerns over the sustainability of gross margins and the lasting impact of tariffs and government activity.

Final Takeaway

Ethan Allen management highlighted the resilience of its vertically integrated structure and strong North American manufacturing base amid significant macroeconomic and tariff challenges. While the company faced lower demand and order declines during the government shutdown, early signs of recovery in January, disciplined expense management, and a shift to digital marketing are expected to support future growth and margin stability. The company remains focused on leveraging talent, technology, and strategic pricing to mitigate ongoing risks and maintain its competitive edge.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UMB Financial signals continued double-digit loan growth and positive operating leverage through 2026 post-acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UMB Financial Corporation (UMBF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO J. Kemper opened by highlighting "another strong quarter to close out 2025, with the successful acquisition of Heartland Financial, the opening of our first branch location in Utah and another year of record earnings." Kemper emphasized "significant improvements in our profitability metrics" and noted the company's "unwavering focus on strong asset quality metrics."
  • Key performance metrics cited by Kemper included a return on average assets of $120 million compared to $104 million in the third quarter and a return on average common equity of 11.27%, up from 10.14%. The efficiency ratio improved to 55.5% from 58.1% in the previous quarter. Reported net income available for common shareholders for the quarter was $209.5 million or $2.74 per share, up 16.1% from Q3. Excluding acquisition and nonrecurring items, net operating income was $235.2 million or $3.08 per share.
  • Kemper highlighted the acquisition of Heartland Financial as a major strategic milestone, stating, "We posted 13% linked quarter annualized growth in average loans and 5.6% in average deposits." He emphasized that C&I lending was the "strongest contributor this quarter with 27% annualized growth over the third quarter average balances," and projected ongoing momentum: "Looking ahead into the first quarter, overall loan activity and pipeline remains strong."
  • On M&A, Kemper stated, "We don't need to do M&A. We have a strong, proven ability to generate assets, and we continue to take share and grow organically at a pace ahead of our peers." He reiterated organic growth as the top capital priority, but left the door open for "tuck-in acquisitions that make financial and strategic sense."
  • CFO Ram Shankar reported, "Our fourth quarter results included $52.7 million in net interest income from purchase accounting adjustments, $12.3 million of which was related to accelerated accretion from early payoffs of acquired loans." He added, "Fee income, excluding these valuation changes was $196.2 million, a decrease of $11.2 million from the third quarter."

Outlook

  • Management expects to achieve positive operating leverage in 2026, despite an estimated $38 million lower contractual purchase accounting accretion benefit and approximately $30 million benefit from investment gains recognized in 2025.
  • First quarter operating expense is expected to be in the $385 million to $390 million range, with an estimated additional $15 million increase in payroll-related costs, offset by expected declines of about $10 million in the second quarter.
  • Shankar stated, "Relative to the fourth quarter, adjusted margin of 2.92% that excludes accretion and the nonrecurring 4 basis points from interest reversals on nonaccrual and the bond prepayment that I mentioned, we expect first quarter margins to be relatively flat."
  • The effective tax rate for 2026 is anticipated to be between 20% and 22%.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income totaled $522.5 million, a 10% increase from the third quarter. Quarterly top line loan production reached $2.6 billion.
  • Trust and securities processing income increased by $4.5 million, or 5.1% linked quarter, driven by new business activities from fund services and private wealth teams.
  • Total net charge-offs for the quarter were 13 basis points, and total nonperforming loans were $145 million or 37 basis points of loans. Total criticized loan levels improved 9.1% from the prior quarter.
  • Excluding merger and one-time costs, operating noninterest expense was $391.8 million, up 1.8% compared to the third quarter.

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets, asked about commercial loan growth drivers and Heartland's contribution. Kemper responded that all markets and verticals performed well, with 50% of growth from new customer acquisition and market share gains. He cited energy and M&A activity as strong contributors. Rine added, "Our franchise lending group has been additive... nice growth from the team out in California as it relates to our ag business."
  • Arfstrom also asked about NPL increase. Terry explained, "The increase was specific to one credit that is fully secured. We don't anticipate any loss from that one."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays, inquired about DDA growth. Shankar said, "We did have some new client acquisition, particularly in our corporate trust group... about $1 billion of inflows coming in, in December."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW, questioned expense saves and investment. Shankar confirmed, "100% of the cost saves that we identified at the time of the announcement of the transaction have been realized as of today." Kemper said, "The operating leverage is where we're focused."
  • Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi, asked about margin stability with growth. Shankar stated, "Generally, I would say the margin will be stable, all else being equal, right, without rate movements, without mix shift in DDA, our earning assets."
  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley, asked about Heartland revenue synergies. Kemper pointed to mortgage, credit card, and corporate trust as key areas, with early positive signs in each.
  • Brian Foran, Truist, questioned lessons learned from the Heartland deal. Kemper described the integration as "flawless," emphasizing the importance of culture and control. Rine agreed, adding, "There's a lot of built-up muscle memory."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was generally positive, with questions focused on drivers of growth, expense saves, and integration synergies. There was little evidence of skepticism or pressing on negative issues.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases such as "I'm incredibly proud of our associates" and "We expect these trends to continue." The tone remained consistent with the previous quarter, though slightly more celebratory regarding the Heartland acquisition's success.
  • Compared to Q3, analyst tone shifted to more granular inquiries about growth drivers and future synergies, reflecting increased confidence in the integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw higher reported net income ($209.5 million vs. $180.4 million) and a stronger efficiency ratio (55.5% vs. 58.1%). Loan growth accelerated (13% annualized vs. 8% in Q3) and C&I lending was particularly robust (27% annualized growth vs. 14% prior quarter).
  • Management's confidence increased, with greater emphasis on the successful integration of Heartland and the scalability of growth. Discussions around operating leverage and cost saves became more concrete, with 100% of identified cost savings now realized.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from integration progress in Q3 to future growth drivers, expense investment, and incremental revenue opportunities in Q4.
  • Strategic focus advanced from initial post-acquisition consolidation in Q3 to leveraging the expanded footprint for organic growth and cross-selling in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited market-related variances impacting noninterest income, including valuation changes in Voyager stock.
  • The increase in NPLs was attributed to a single fully secured credit, with no anticipated loss.
  • Shankar mentioned "very limited line of sight" into episodic deposit inflows, which could affect future deposit growth predictability.
  • There is ongoing assessment of M&A opportunities, but management remains cautious about deals that could approach the $100 billion asset threshold due to regulatory uncertainties.

Final Takeaway

UMB Financial delivered a record quarter, driven by double-digit annualized loan growth, robust C&I performance, and the successful integration of Heartland Financial. Management underscored a continued focus on organic growth, operating leverage, and asset quality, with positive outlooks for further expansion in acquired and existing markets. The company highlighted that all targeted cost synergies from the Heartland deal have now been achieved, positioning UMB to capitalize on new opportunities and maintain momentum into 2026, while keeping a disciplined approach to both organic and inorganic growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comstock plunges after launching public stock offering
data.description:
Comstock (LODE) -13.3% post-market Wednesday after saying it commenced a public offering for the sale of its common stock, with an underwriter's option to purchase additional common stock up to 15% of the shares in the offering; the size of the offering was not specified.

Comstock (LODE) said it plans to use the proceeds from the offering to fund capital expenditure requirements for Comstock Metals related to its second industry-scale facility, the development of a refining process and solution, accelerated site selections and Metals market growth, and for general corporate purposes.

Titan Partners, a division of American Capital Partners, is acting as the sole bookrunner for the offering.

data.title: Tesla beats Q4 expectations, gives upbeat outlook on robotaxi, Optimus - update
data.description:
Updated 18:00 ET with analyst comments

Tesla (TSLA) shares are gaining ground as a top- and bottom-line beat coupled with an upbeat outlook on Cybercab and robotaxi growth overshadowed another quarterly revenue decline, reflecting the end of government tax incentives and heightened competition in China and Europe.

In the fourth quarter, Tesla (TSLA) earned an adjusted profit of $0.50 per share, down 17% from a year ago but 5 cents better than expectations and unchanged from the prior quarter. Additionally, adjusted EBITDA was down 4% to $4.15B, while adjusted EBITDA margin dropped 17 basis points to 16.7%. The company’s operating margin was compressed by 50 basis points to 5.7% as an 11% decrease in operating income was matched by a 39% jump in operating expenses.

An 11% decline in total automotive revenue was partially offset by a sizable gain in energy generation and storage (+25%), leading to a 3% drop in revenue for the quarter to an above consensus $24.9B (versus $24.8B estimates).

While automotive sales declined sequentially, gross margin (even when excluding the impact of regulatory credits) improved. The company also noted that it achieved its highest quarterly energy storage deployment, driven by record Megapack deployments.

On robotics, the company said it continued to make progress on its Optimus program with plans to unveil the Gen 3 version of Optimus in the first quarter of this year to include major upgrades from version 2.5.

Tesla (TSLA) began removing the safety monitor from its driverless robotaxis this month on a limited basis, unlocking further expansion of the robotaxi fleet and coverage in the Austin-metro area.

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) were up nearly 3% in the wake of Q4 results, supporting modest gains for peers NIO (NIO), General Motors (GM), Rivian Automotive (RIVN), and Lucid Group (LCID).

Seeking Alpha analyst Jonathan Weber, Investing Group Leader for Cash Flow Club: Tesla fared better than feared, with the company beating estimates on both lines. But its results still looked bad—revenues declined, margins declined, and profits continued to fall. For a stock that trades at a very high valuation, worsening fundamentals and a lack of growth are a problem, making TSLA look unappealing for now.

Seeking Alpha analyst Brett Ashcroft Green: With another full year of delivery declines, lower GAAP EPS, and revenue contraction, we would assume these to be negatives. Smaller segments, like energy storage, continued to grow at a record 14.2 GWh for FY 2025. FSD and robotaxis are the big story that still keep investors driving this higher, anticipating future, yet-to-be-seen growth. If Musk can keep selling the story, the stock can trend higher regardless of worsening fundamentals. There are few, if any, stocks like it, a pure bet that Tesla will dominate every aspect of physical AI.

Seeking Alpha analyst Geneva Investor: After Q4 earnings, it is clearer than ever how Tesla is positioning itself as a "physical AI company." This means I do expect heavy CapEx investment for 2026, and in this CapEx-heavy context, Tesla will benefit from a weaker U.S. dollar and lower interest rates. Overall, both the macro context and Tesla's Q4 double beat reassure me Musk is executing on his vision of a fully automated world. I remain bullish. 

ETFs that are leveraged to Tesla's (TSLA) share price include the Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares (TSLL) ETF, GraniteShares 2x Long Tesla Daily ETF (TSLR), ProShares Ultra TSLA ETF (TSLI), and Simplify Volt TSLA Revolution ETF (TESL). Other ETFs with a high weighting of Tesla (TSLA) include Global X Purecap MSCI Consumer Discretionary ETF (GXPD), Grayscale Bitcoin Adopters ETF (BCOR), the Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (XLY), and the Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (FDIS).
